Source Title: The 9/11 Commission Report (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: NY Fed estimate of direct costs (earnings losses, property damage, cleanup) $33–$36B (through June 2002); using midpoint.
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was weak because fragmented intelligence signals were not integrated into a clear threat picture. Agility was mixed: first responders mobilized quickly, but communications and interoperability problems limited coordination. Resilience improved afterward through major reforms in homeland security, aviation screening, and intelligence sharing. The lesson for decision-makers is that early warning systems only create value when agencies can fuse information and act on it quickly.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Benchmark estimate using national market reopening and long-horizon physical/institutional rebuild.

Source Title: Columbia Accident Investigation Board Report Vol. 1 (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: NASA estimate for return-to-flight costs following Columbia accident; proxy for program impact (not CPI-adjusted).
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was mixed because foam-strike risk had been observed previously but was not treated as a mission-stopping hazard. Agility during the mission was limited because decision-makers lacked a viable in-flight recovery option and underestimated the damage. Resilience was strong afterward, with NASA changing inspection, imaging, and shuttle flight readiness processes. The decision lesson is to elevate known technical anomalies before they become catastrophic failures.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Program-level recovery based on shuttle stand-down and gradual return-to-flight horizon.

Category Bucket: Non-weather infrastructure disruptions (electrical + cyber + fuel + critical systems)
Source Title: Final Report on the Aug 14, 2003 Blackout (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: Commonly cited economic cost range $7–$10B; using midpoint (not CPI-adjusted).
Cost Source (URL/Citation): (cost range widely cited; add primary report later)
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was only partial because operators had monitoring tools, but cascading-failure risk was underestimated and tree-trimming weaknesses persisted. Agility was moderate because utilities restored power quickly in some areas, but coordination failures slowed stabilization. Resilience improved through mandatory reliability standards and stronger grid oversight. Leaders should treat infrastructure maintenance and cross-operator coordination as prevention investments, not optional costs.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Grid service restored quickly; secondary economic normalization extended about two weeks.

Source Title: The Federal Response to Hurricane Katrina: Lessons Learned (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: NOAA reports event-level loss estimates; see methodology on NCEI site.
Notes: Cost from NOAA NCEI Billion-Dollar Weather and Climate Disasters (CPI-adjusted to 2024; values in millions).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was undermined by long-known levee vulnerabilities even though storm forecasting itself was strong. Agility was weak because response and evacuation coordination across local, state, and federal levels was slow and inconsistent. Resilience improved later through FEMA reforms and major levee investments, but only after enormous human and economic loss. The lesson is that accurate forecasting is not enough if structural vulnerabilities are left unresolved.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Essential services returned unevenly over months; full housing/infrastructure recovery took years.

Source Title: Hurricanes Katrina and Rita After Action Report & Improvement Plan (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: NOAA reports event-level loss estimates; see methodology on NCEI site.
Notes: Cost from NOAA NCEI Billion-Dollar Weather and Climate Disasters (CPI-adjusted to 2024; values in millions).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: This after-action record shows stronger vigilance than Katrina because emergency planning and evacuation improved across the Gulf Coast. Agility benefited from lessons learned, though transportation bottlenecks and sheltering pressures still created stress. Resilience was stronger because organizations were able to apply prior disaster lessons more effectively. The decision lesson is that organizations can materially improve readiness when after-action findings are actually institutionalized.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: AAR/IP duplicate benchmark aligned to Gulf Coast multi-year restoration horizon.

Source Title: Virginia Tech Review Panel Report (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: Center for American Progress report estimate at least $48.2M total (taxpayers + university + donors).
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was weak because the attacker’s warning signs were not translated into preventive action. Agility was moderate as campus and law-enforcement response occurred, but not before mass casualties were sustained. Resilience improved through changes in campus alert systems, emergency procedures, and threat-assessment practices. Leaders should invest in behavioral threat detection and rapid-notification systems before crises occur.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Campus operations stabilized rapidly; institutional/community recovery extended through semester scale.

Agility was relatively strong: emergency responders mobilized rapidly and rescue operations were immediate. Resilience improved through bridge inspection reform and infrastructure investment reviews. The key lesson is that asset-monitoring systems must be linked to timely maintenance and decision action.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Traffic workarounds came quickly; full transportation recovery waited on bridge replacement.

Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).

Agility was moderate to strong as vaccination campaigns, public guidance, and health-system responses were deployed quickly. Resilience improved through stronger pandemic planning and surveillance practices, although uneven readiness remained. Decision-makers should treat public-health monitoring and communication capacity as core resilience infrastructure.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Public health stabilization occurred within season; broader normalization took about a year.

Source Title: National Commission on BP Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: BP cumulative pre-tax charge estimate; relates to cleanup + legal liabilities (not CPI-adjusted).
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was weak because warning signs related to drilling safety, well integrity, and risk controls were not acted on adequately. Agility was limited because containment and response were slowed by the technical complexity of the blowout. Resilience improved through regulatory reform and stronger offshore drilling oversight, but environmental recovery was prolonged. The lesson is that high-risk industrial operations require aggressive prevention and safety culture, not just response planning.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Leak control took months; ecological and economic recovery extended for years.

Resilience was also strong because rebuilding incorporated improved building standards and preparedness practices. Leaders should remember that warning quality matters less if the public cannot convert alerts into action.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Emergency stabilization was fast, but full housing/business rebuild extended multiple years.

Category Bucket: Non-weather infrastructure disruptions (electrical + cyber + fuel + critical systems)

Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was weak to moderate because grid interconnection risk and equipment failure potential were not fully anticipated. Agility was mixed: restoration efforts moved quickly, but the regional nature of the outage exposed coordination limits. Resilience improved through technical and operational reviews of grid protection systems. The lesson is that tightly coupled infrastructure demands both redundancy and cross-jurisdictional incident playbooks.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Most service came back within days; downstream normalization within the following week.

Source Title: CSB Investigation: West Fertilizer Fire & Explosion (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: Insurance Council of Texas estimate ~ $100M in damages (not CPI-adjusted).
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was weak because known fertilizer storage and fire risks were not managed effectively before the explosion. Agility was limited by the speed and violence of the event, though local responders acted quickly. Resilience improved through greater national attention to chemical storage and industrial safety oversight. Leaders should focus on prevention, inspection, and hazardous-material controls, especially where public exposure is high.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Immediate stabilization and debris clearance were weeks; community/infrastructure recovery about a year.

Source Title: CDC's Response to the 2014–2016 Ebola Epidemic (MMWR PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: Emergency Ebola appropriation provided about $5.4B (funding, not total economic cost).
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was stronger than in many prior outbreaks because international surveillance identified the Ebola threat early, but domestic preparedness gaps still appeared. Agility improved as CDC and health systems rapidly built screening, tracing, and treatment protocols. Resilience increased through stronger infectious-disease preparedness and coordination procedures. The lesson is that rare but high-consequence health threats require prebuilt surge protocols, not ad hoc improvisation.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Initial containment and response scale-up were weeks; sustained recovery and monitoring lasted years.

Source Title: CDC Strategy for Global Response to COVID-19 (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: Cutler & Summers estimate of total cost (lost output + health losses), described as “more than $16T” (not CPI-adjusted).
Notes: Cost value is nominal per cited source (not CPI-adjusted to 2024).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was mixed because early global warning signs existed, but domestic readiness and interpretation varied across institutions. Agility was uneven: some organizations moved quickly on testing, distancing, and emergency operations, while others struggled with coordination and supply chains. Resilience improved through vaccines, better public-health infrastructure, and expanded preparedness doctrine, but gaps remained. The decision lesson is that complex crises reward organizations that can act under uncertainty and learn quickly.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Initial stabilization required months; full societal and system normalization was multi-year.

Event Name: Texas Winter Storm Uri / power crisis
Source Title: FERC/NERC Event Report: Feb 2021 Cold Weather Outages (PDF)
Cost Range / Notes: NOAA reports event-level loss estimates; see methodology on NCEI site.
Notes: Cost from NOAA NCEI Billion-Dollar Weather and Climate Disasters (CPI-adjusted to 2024; values in millions).
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance was weak because prior warnings about winterization and cold-weather grid risk had been raised but not fully addressed. Agility was moderate at best: operators used rolling blackouts, but cascading failures overwhelmed the system. Resilience improved afterward through reform pressure, winterization requirements, and reliability reviews. Leaders should see this as a case where prevention and infrastructure hardening would have been more valuable than reactive crisis management.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Basic utility restoration occurred over days to weeks; full remediation/reliability changes extended about a year.

The lesson is that inspection regimes must be designed to surface low-frequency but catastrophic infrastructure risks.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Rescue-to-stabilization was weeks; legal, housing, and community recovery extended multiple years.

Recovery Basis / Assumption: Mail-screening and facility reopening took weeks; full institutional confidence/process recovery about a year.

Source Title: CDC mpox U.S. response archive
VAR Insight / Lessons Learned: Vigilance involves early disease surveillance and public health monitoring systems. Agility reflects the speed of testing, treatment protocols, and public health interventions once the threat emerges. Resilience is demonstrated through vaccine development, healthcare system adaptation, and improved preparedness planning. The lesson is that strong surveillance and coordinated health response systems are essential for managing large-scale outbreaks.
Recovery Basis / Assumption: Case decline and targeted vaccination improved conditions over weeks; fuller normalization over months.
